docs.title: Junos SP Training Labs
docs.text: These labs are designed for adult professionals learning Juniper Networks service provider concepts using vMX (Junos 14.1, VCP-only) in GNS3. The series maps to the JNCIS-SP (JN0-362) exam objectives and assumes you have already completed a CCNA-level routing & switching foundation.

docs.title: What You Will Build
docs.text:
By the end of this series you will have configured and verified a complete MPLS service provider network from scratch — including IGP, BGP, LDP, and L3 VPNs — using the same Junos CLI and configuration hierarchy used in production ISP environments.

graph LR
  CE1([CE1<br/>AS 65100]) --- PE1([PE1<br/>10.0.0.1])
  PE1 --- P1([P1<br/>10.0.0.2])
  P1 --- P2([P2<br/>10.0.0.3])
  P2 --- PE2([PE2<br/>10.0.0.4])
  PE2 --- CE2([CE2<br/>AS 65100])

docs.title: Lab Series
docs.text: Session Topic Key Skills Session 1 GNS3 Platform & Junos CLI vMX setup, operational/config modes, commit/rollback Session 2 Interfaces & Static Routing ge-0/0/X config, family inet, loopbacks, static routes Session 3 Bridging & VLANs Bridge domains, 802.1Q trunks, access ports, IRB inter-VLAN routing Session 3a Spanning Tree Protocol (self-directed) RSTP, root election, port roles, failover Session 4 OSPF Single-Area Area 0, neighbor adjacency, LSAs, passive interfaces Session 5 IS-IS NET addressing, Level 2 single-area, IS-IS adjacency Session 6 BGP eBGP PE-CE, iBGP PE-PE, next-hop-self, prefix propagation Session 7 MPLS & LDP Label switching, LDP neighbor, RSVP-TE intro Session 7a Layer 2 Services (self-directed) L2circuit, VPLS Session 8 BGP/MPLS L3 VPN VRF, route distinguisher, route target, MP-BGP Session 9 Capstone Troubleshooting Multi-fault diagnosis across all layers

docs.title: Technical Requirements
docs.text:
Component Requirement GNS3 2.2.44 or later GNS3 VM 2.2.44 (VMware Workstation or VirtualBox) vMX image Junos 14.1R4.8 hda.qcow2 (from Juniper support portal) RAM per node 2048 MB vCPUs per node 1 Nodes per lab 4–6 (16–24 GB host RAM recommended) Host OS Windows 10/11 or Linux

Boot time & first boot reboot

vMX takes 3–5 minutes to boot. Do not attempt configuration until the root@% prompt appears. On the first boot of a fresh image, vMX prints a network-services warning and requires an immediate request system reboot before proceeding — this is normal and only happens once.

docs.title: Key Differences from Cisco IOS
docs.text:
If you are coming from the Cisco CCNA lab series, these are the most important Junos concepts to internalize before starting:

Cisco IOS Junos OS interface GigabitEthernet0/0 set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 ... ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0 set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet address 10.1.1.1/24 no shutdown (required) Interfaces are enabled by default show ip route show route show ip ospf neighbor show ospf neighbor Running-config is live Config is staged — commit to apply copy run start Not needed — commit persists to storage ACLs Firewall filters with named terms VRF Routing instance (instance-type vrf)

The commit model

Junos uses a two-stage configuration model. All changes you type are staged in a candidate configuration. Nothing changes on the router until you run commit. You can always discard with rollback 0 or revert to the last good state with rollback 1.

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session you will be able to:

  • Download and import the vMX 14.1 QEMU image into GNS3
  • Create a two-router GNS3 project and boot both nodes successfully
  • Navigate Junos operational mode and configuration mode
  • Apply, verify, and roll back a basic configuration
  • Use show | compare to review staged changes before committing

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• GNS3 2.2.44+ and GNS3 VM installed
  • Virtualization enabled in BIOS (VT-x / AMD-V)
  • ~3 GB free disk space for the vMX image
  • Juniper account (free) to download the image

docs.title: Topology Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
  R1([R1<br/>vMX-14.1]) ---|ge-0/0/0| R2([R2<br/>vMX-14.1])

Two routers connected back-to-back. No routing protocol yet — the goal is platform familiarity.

docs.title: Session Parts
docs.text: Part Topic Part 0 Download & import the vMX-14.1 image Part 1 Create a GNS3 project and add devices Part 2 Connect devices and boot Part 3 Junos CLI fundamentals Verification Checklist

docs.title: GNS3 Requirements
docs.text: Component Minimum Recommended GNS3 2.2.44 Latest stable GNS3 VM 2.2.44 (must match GNS3) Latest stable Hypervisor VMware Workstation Player (free) VMware Workstation Pro GNS3 VM RAM 8 GB 16 GB GNS3 VM vCPUs 4 8 GNS3 VM Disk 50 GB 100 GB

docs.title: vMX Image
docs.text: Property Value Image name hda.qcow2 (vMX 14.1R4.8) Source support.juniper.net — free with Juniper account Format QCOW2 (QEMU disk image) Disk size ~2 GB RAM per node 2048 MB vCPUs per node 1 Boot time 3–5 minutes

docs.title: QEMU Template Settings
docs.text: Setting Value RAM 2048 MB vCPUs 1 HDD interface ide NIC type virtio-net-pci NIC count 6 Additional QEMU options -serial mon:stdio -nographic -M pc KVM Required

docs.title: Adapter to Interface Mapping
docs.text:
GNS3 Adapter Junos Interface Notes Adapter 0 em0 External management Adapter 1 em1 Internal (pre-configured 172.16.0.1/16) Adapter 2 ge-0/0/0 First lab interface Adapter 3 ge-0/0/1 Second lab interface Adapter 4 ge-0/0/2 Third lab interface Adapter 5 ge-0/0/3 Fourth lab interface

Always connect Adapter 2+ for lab links

Adapters 0 and 1 are reserved by vMX for management. All GNS3 topology links must use Adapter 2 or higher.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
  R1([R1<br/>vMX-14.1]) ---|ge-0/0/0| R2([R2<br/>vMX-14.1])

docs.title: Device Summary
docs.text: Device Role Image R1 Router vMX (Junos 14.1R4.8) R2 Router vMX (Junos 14.1R4.8)

docs.title: Link Summary
docs.text: Link R1 Interface R2 Interface R1 — R2 ge-0/0/0 ge-0/0/0

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This session uses only two routers with a single link. The topology expands to four provider routers and two customer routers in Session 3 and remains constant from Session 3 through Session 8.

No IP addressing is configured in this session. Interface configuration begins in Session 2.

docs.title: Mode Navigation
docs.text: Command Description cli Enter Junos CLI from BSD shell configure Enter configuration mode exit Exit current mode (config to operational, or operational to shell) quit Same as exit

docs.title: Show Commands (Operational Mode)
docs.text: Command Description show version Junos version, model, uptime show version \| match Hostname Configured hostname show system uptime Boot time and current uptime show interfaces terse All interfaces — state and IP summary show interfaces ge-0/0/0 Detailed stats for one interface show interfaces ge-0/0/0 detail Full counters and flags show route Routing table show configuration Full committed configuration show configuration \| display set Committed config in set format

docs.title: Configuration Mode Commands
docs.text: Command Description set <path> <value> Stage a configuration change delete <path> Stage a configuration deletion edit <path> Navigate into a config hierarchy level up Go up one level in the hierarchy top Return to top of hierarchy show Show candidate config at current level show \| compare Diff: candidate vs committed (+ added, - removed) show \| display set Candidate config in set format commit Apply all staged changes commit confirmed <minutes> Apply changes with auto-rollback timer commit confirmed then commit Confirm a commit confirmed (disarms timer) rollback 0 Discard all staged changes (back to committed) rollback 1 Load previous committed config as candidate rollback ? List available rollback generations

docs.title: Session 1 Wrap-Up
docs.text:
The Junos commit model is a fundamental shift from Cisco IOS, where every command takes effect immediately. In Junos:

  1. You build changes in the candidate configuration
  2. You review them with show | compare
  3. You apply them with commit
  4. You can always revert with rollback

This model makes Junos significantly safer to configure on production equipment, but it requires an extra mental step compared to IOS. Getting comfortable with this workflow in Session 1 pays dividends through every session that follows.

docs.title: GNS3 VM Not Detected
docs.text:
Symptom: GNS3 shows "GNS3 VM is not running" or the VM status is red.

Cause / Fix:

  1. Open VMware and confirm the GNS3 VM is powered on
  2. In GNS3: Edit > Preferences > GNS3 VM — verify the VM IP appears
  3. If the IP field is empty, restart the GNS3 VM and click the refresh button
  4. Confirm the GNS3 app version matches the GNS3 VM version exactly

docs.title: vMX Node Shows Red X
docs.text:
Symptom: After starting nodes, one or both nodes show a red X instead of green.

Cause / Fix:

  1. Right-click the node > Show log — look for QEMU errors
  2. Most common cause: nested virtualization not enabled in VMware settings
  3. Shut down the GNS3 VM
  4. VM Settings > Processors > enable "Virtualize Intel VT-x/EPT or AMD-V/RVI"
  5. Restart the GNS3 VM and try again
  6. Second common cause: insufficient RAM — confirm GNS3 VM has at least 8 GB

docs.title: Boot Hangs at mount /dev/vda /boot or mount /dev/hda1 /boot
docs.text:
Symptom: The console shows boot messages but freezes at a mount line.

Cause: QEMU 8.x defaults to the q35 machine type. vMX was built for the older pc (i440FX) machine type and stalls during boot when q35 is used.

Fix:

  1. Stop the node (right-click > Stop)
  2. Go to Edit > Preferences > QEMU > QEMU VMs
  3. Select vMX-14.1 and click Edit
  4. Go to the Advanced tab
  5. Confirm Additional settings contains -M pc:
-serial mon:stdio -nographic -M pc
  1. Click OK, then start the node again

docs.title: First Boot Warning: "Chassis configuration for network services has been changed"
docs.text:
Symptom: On first boot, the console prints:

WARNING: Chassis configuration for network services has been changed.
A system reboot is mandatory. Please reboot the system NOW.

Cause: This is expected on a fresh image — vMX is setting its network-services mode for the first time.

Fix: This is a one-time initialization. Log in as root, type cli, then reboot:

request system reboot

Confirm with yes. The warning will not appear on subsequent boots of the same node instance.

docs.title: Console Shows No Output / Stays Blank
docs.text:
Symptom: Console opens but nothing appears.

Cause / Fix:

  1. Wait — vMX takes 3–5 minutes to boot; a blank console is normal for the first 60–90 seconds
  2. Press Enter once — this sometimes wakes a stalled console
  3. If still blank after 10 minutes, right-click the node > Stop, then Start again

docs.title: ge-0/0/0 Not Reachable After Connecting Nodes
docs.text:
Symptom: You connected two nodes and configured ge-0/0/0 but pings fail.

Cause: The link was drawn using Adapter 0 or Adapter 1 instead of Adapter 2. vMX reserves the first two adapters for management — ge-0/0/0 maps to Adapter 2.

Fix: Delete the link in GNS3, redraw it connecting Adapter 2 on each node.

docs.title: Hostname Does Not Change After commit
docs.text:
Symptom: You set system host-name R1 and committed, but the prompt still shows root.

Fix: Exit and re-enter the CLI:

[edit]
root# exit
root> exit
root@% cli
R1>

docs.title: Step 1: Install GNS3 and GNS3 VM
docs.text:
1. Download the GNS3 all-in-one installer from gns3.com/software
  2. Run the installer — select GNS3 VM when prompted to choose a VM type
  3. Download the GNS3 VM .ova file from the same download page (version must match GNS3)
  4. Import the .ova into VMware Workstation Player:
  5. File > Open > select the .ova
  6. Accept defaults; the VM will appear in your library
  7. In GNS3, go to Edit > Preferences > GNS3 VM and confirm the VM is detected

Match versions exactly

The GNS3 application and GNS3 VM version numbers must match (e.g., both 2.2.44). Mismatched versions cause connection errors that are difficult to diagnose.

docs.title: Step 2: Configure GNS3 VM Resources
docs.text:
In GNS3, go to Edit > Preferences > GNS3 VM and set the resources:

  • RAM: 8 GB minimum (each vMX node needs 2 GB)
  • vCPUs: 4 or more

Click OK to apply. GNS3 will restart the VM with the updated resources.

Nested virtualization — VMware setting

QEMU requires hardware virtualization support. In VMware Workstation, open the GNS3 VM's settings, go to Processors, and enable "Virtualize Intel VT-x/EPT or AMD-V/RVI". This must be set before starting the GNS3 VM.

Start the GNS3 VM and wait for it to show its IP address before launching GNS3.

docs.title: Step 3: Obtain the vMX Image
docs.text:
These labs use the Juniper vMX 14.1 image (hda.qcow2). The image is available from:

  • Juniper Software Portal — support.juniper.net (free account required) > Downloads > Junos Platforms > Virtual > vMX
  • Existing EVE-NG installation — copy from /opt/unetlab/addons/qemu/vmx-14.1R<x>.<y>/hda.qcow2

docs.title: Step 4: Create the GNS3 QEMU Template
docs.text:
1. In GNS3, go to Edit > Preferences > QEMU > QEMU VMs > New
  2. Set Name: vMX-14.1
  3. Set RAM: 2048
  4. Leave QEMU binary as default (qemu-system-x86_64)
  5. Click Next, select New Image, browse to your hda.qcow2 — GNS3 uploads it to the GNS3 VM automatically
  6. Click Finish

Then right-click the template and choose Edit:

Tab Setting Value General vCPUs 1 HDD Disk interface ide Network Adapters 6 Network Type virtio-net-pci Advanced Additional settings -serial mon:stdio -nographic -M pc Advanced Use KVM if available checked

Machine type must be pc

The -M pc flag forces the legacy i440FX machine type. Without it, QEMU 8.x defaults to q35 and vMX hangs at mount /dev/vda /boot on every boot.

Why 6 adapters?

vMX reserves the first two adapters for management interfaces — Adapter 0 becomes em0 (external management) and Adapter 1 becomes em1 (internal, pre-configured at 172.16.0.1/16). Data plane interfaces start at Adapter 2. Six adapters gives you ge-0/0/0 through ge-0/0/3 for lab use.

GNS3 Adapter Junos Interface Adapter 0 em0 (management) Adapter 1 em1 (internal) Adapter 2 ge-0/0/0 Adapter 3 ge-0/0/1 Adapter 4 ge-0/0/2 Adapter 5 ge-0/0/3

Click OK to save the template.

docs.title: Step 5: First Boot Procedure
docs.text:
vMX requires a one-time reboot on first boot to apply its network-services configuration. This only happens once per fresh image instance.

  1. Drag a vMX-14.1 node onto the canvas and start it
  2. Open the console — wait 3–5 minutes for boot to complete
  3. At the login prompt, type root and press Enter (no password on a fresh image)
  4. Type cli to enter the Junos CLI from the BSD shell:
--- JUNOS 14.1R4.8 built 2015-01-28 03:38:12 UTC
root@% cli
root>
  1. You will see a warning during first boot:
WARNING: Chassis configuration for network services has been changed.
A system reboot is mandatory. Please reboot the system NOW.
  1. Reboot immediately:
request system reboot

Confirm with yes. After the second boot the warning will not appear again.

docs.title: Step 6: CLI Setup
docs.text:
After the second boot, log back in (root / cli) and run these two preferences before entering configuration mode:

set cli screen-length 0
set cli complete-on-space off

screen-length 0 disables the --More-- pager so show output never pauses mid-screen. complete-on-space off prevents the spacebar from triggering keyword completion when pasting multi-word commands.

These do not persist across reconnects

Re-run both commands at the start of every console session. They apply to the current session only.

Pasting large config blocks

Use load set terminal in config mode instead of pasting directly at the # prompt. Junos buffers all input until you press Ctrl+D, then processes every set command at once — eliminating paste-rate issues with console terminals.

configure
load set terminal
set system host-name PE1
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 ...
^D
commit

docs.title: Part 1 — Create a GNS3 Project & Add Devices
Step 1: Create a New Project
docs.text:
1. In GNS3, select File > New blank project
  2. Name it JNCIS-SP-Session1
  3. Click OK

The project opens on a blank canvas.

docs.title: Step 2: Add Two vMX Nodes
docs.text:
1. In the Devices panel on the left, expand Routers
  2. Drag vMX-14.1 onto the canvas twice
  3. GNS3 names them vMX-14.1-1 and vMX-14.1-2 automatically

Rename for clarity

Right-click each node, choose Change hostname, and rename them R1 and R2. These names appear on the canvas and on the Junos console prompt after configuration.

docs.title: Step 3: Review Node Properties
docs.text:
Right-click R1 and choose Configure to confirm:

  • RAM: 2048 MB
  • Adapters: 6
  • The hda.qcow2 disk image is attached

Repeat for R2.

docs.title: Step 4: Save the Project
docs.text: Press Ctrl+S or use File > Save project. Save frequently — GNS3 project state can be lost if the VM crashes mid-session.

docs.title: Step 1: Draw the Link
docs.text:
1. In GNS3, click the Add a link button (cable icon in the toolbar)
  2. Click R1 — a menu pops up showing its adapters
  3. Select Adapter 2 (this maps to ge-0/0/0 inside Junos)
  4. Click R2 — select Adapter 2

A line appears between the two nodes.

Always use Adapter 2 for ge-0/0/0

vMX reserves Adapter 0 (em0) and Adapter 1 (em1) for internal management. Data plane interfaces start at Adapter 2. Connecting Adapter 0 or 1 between nodes will not produce ge-0/0/0 connectivity.

GNS3 Adapter Junos Interface Adapter 0 em0 — management only Adapter 1 em1 — internal (172.16.0.1/16) Adapter 2 ge-0/0/0 Adapter 3 ge-0/0/1 Adapter 4 ge-0/0/2 Adapter 5 ge-0/0/3

docs.title: Step 2: Start the Nodes
docs.text:
Click the green Start all nodes button or right-click each node and choose Start.

Boot time: 3–5 minutes

vMX loads a full Junos OS. Do not open a console or attempt configuration until the node is fully booted.

docs.title: Step 3: Open the Console and Log In
docs.text:
Once both nodes show green links:

  1. Right-click R1 > Console
  2. Wait for the login prompt, then type root and press Enter — no password required on a fresh image
  3. Type cli to enter the Junos CLI:
--- JUNOS 14.1R4.8 built 2015-01-28 03:38:12 UTC
root@% cli
root>

The > prompt indicates you are in operational mode.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify the Node is Ready
docs.text:
show version

Expected output:

Junos: 14.1R4.8
...
Model: vmx

show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse

Expected:

Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
ge-0/0/0                up    up

If ge-0/0/0 shows up up the node is ready for configuration.

ge-0/0/x not visible in show interfaces terse

On vMX 14.1, ge-0/0/x interfaces do not appear in the general show interfaces terse output until they have an IP address configured. Query them directly with show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse to confirm they exist.

docs.title: Part 3 — Junos CLI Fundamentals
docs.text: This part covers the core Junos concepts you will use in every subsequent session. Take your time here — understanding the commit model is the single most important prerequisite for working in Junos.

docs.title: The Two CLI Modes
docs.text:
Junos has two distinct CLI modes:

Mode Prompt Purpose Operational root> Read-only monitoring — show, ping, traceroute Configuration root# Staged editing — all changes go to a candidate config

Switch between them:

root> configure
Entering configuration mode

[edit]
root#

[edit]
root# exit
root>

docs.title: The Candidate Configuration
docs.text:
This is the most important Junos concept

Every change you make in configuration mode modifies a candidate configuration — a draft copy that has no effect on the running router until you commit. This means you can make multiple changes, review them with show | compare, and then apply them all at once — or discard them completely with rollback 0.

docs.title: Example: Set the hostname
docs.text:
On R1's console:

root> configure

[edit]
root# set system host-name R1

[edit]
root# show | compare
[edit system]
+ host-name R1;

[edit]
root# commit
commit complete

[edit]
root# exit
R1>

The prompt changed to R1> — the hostname is now live.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If you make a mistake, rollback 1 reverts to the configuration that was in place before your last commit:

[edit]
R1# rollback 1
load complete

[edit]
R1# commit
commit complete

Junos keeps up to 50 rollback generations (rollback 0 through rollback 49).

docs.title: Configuration Hierarchy
docs.text:
Junos configuration is hierarchical — like a nested tree. You navigate it with edit to go deeper and up / top to go back up.

[edit]
R1# edit system

[edit system]
R1# set host-name R1

[edit system]
R1# top

Alternatively, you can issue the full path from anywhere:

[edit]
R1# set system host-name R1

Both are equivalent. Full-path set commands are used throughout these labs for clarity.

docs.title: Useful Operational Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose show version Junos version and platform show interfaces terse All interfaces and their IP addresses show interfaces ge-0/0/0 Detail for a single interface show route Routing table (all protocols) show system uptime System uptime ping 10.1.1.2 ICMP ping traceroute 10.1.1.2 Path trace

docs.title: Useful Configuration Commands
docs.text: Command Purpose configure Enter configuration mode set ... Stage a configuration change delete ... Stage a configuration deletion show \| compare Show pending changes (diff vs committed) commit Apply staged changes commit confirmed 5 Apply changes; auto-rollback in 5 min if not confirmed rollback 0 Discard all staged changes rollback 1 Revert to the previous committed configuration show Show current candidate configuration at this level show \| display set Show candidate config in set command format

docs.title: Task: Configure Both Routers
docs.text:
On R1:

root> configure

[edit]
root# set system host-name R1
root# set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123

[edit]
root# commit
commit complete

On R2 (open R2's console via GNS3):

root> configure

[edit]
root# set system host-name R2
root# set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123

[edit]
root# commit
commit complete

Set a root password

Junos will warn you if you commit without setting a root password. In production this is a safety gate; in the lab it is acceptable to set lab123 as shown above.

docs.title: Session 1 — Verification Checklist
docs.text: Complete these checks before moving to Session 2.

docs.title: Platform
docs.text:
• GNS3 and GNS3 VM are the same version and communicate successfully
  • vMX-14.1 template is created with 6 adapters and 2048 MB RAM
  • Both R1 and R2 boot successfully (Junos root@ prompt appears)

docs.title: CLI Fundamentals
docs.text:
• You can switch between operational mode (>) and configuration mode (#)
  • show version shows Junos 14.1R4.8 and model vmx
  • R1 hostname is set to R1, R2 hostname is set to R2
  • Root password is configured on both routers
  • You have used show | compare to review a change before committing

docs.title: Quick Commands
docs.text:
Run these on each router and confirm the output matches expectations:

show version
Expected: Model: vmx, Junos version 14.1R4.8

show version | match Hostname
Expected: Hostname: R1 (or R2)

show interfaces terse
Expected: em0, em1, lo0 listed. ge-0/0/x interfaces only appear after an IP address is configured — query them directly with show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session you will be able to:

  • Configure Junos physical interfaces with IPv4 addresses
  • Configure loopback interfaces (lo0) — the Junos equivalent of IOS interface Loopback0
  • Verify interface state with show interfaces terse and show interfaces detail
  • Configure static routes under routing-options
  • Configure a default route and verify reachability end-to-end

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Session 1 complete — both R1 and R2 boot and have hostnames set
  • Understanding of Junos operational vs configuration mode and the commit model

docs.title: Addressing Table
docs.text: Device Interface Address Purpose R1 ge-0/0/0 10.1.12.1/30 WAN link to R2 R1 lo0.0 10.0.0.1/32 Loopback (router ID) R2 ge-0/0/0 10.1.12.2/30 WAN link to R1 R2 lo0.0 10.0.0.2/32 Loopback (router ID)

docs.title: Session Parts
docs.text: Part Topic Part 0 Base config (hostname, root password) Part 1 Physical interface addressing Part 2 Loopbacks and static routes Part 3 Default route and verification Verification Checklist

docs.title: Interface Addresses
docs.text: Device Interface IPv4 Address Subnet Purpose R1 ge-0/0/0 unit 0 10.1.12.1 /30 WAN link R1–R2 R1 lo0 unit 0 10.0.0.1 /32 Loopback R2 ge-0/0/0 unit 0 10.1.12.2 /30 WAN link R1–R2 R2 lo0 unit 0 10.0.0.2 /32 Loopback

docs.title: Subnet Reference
docs.text: Subnet Purpose Usable Hosts 10.1.12.0/30 R1 — R2 WAN link .1 (R1), .2 (R2) 10.0.0.1/32 R1 loopback R1 only 10.0.0.2/32 R2 loopback R2 only

docs.title: Static Routes
docs.text:
Device Destination Next-hop Purpose R1 10.0.0.2/32 10.1.12.2 Reach R2 loopback R2 10.0.0.1/32 10.1.12.1 Reach R1 loopback

/30 for point-to-point links

A /30 gives you 4 addresses, 2 usable — perfect for point-to-point router links. These labs use the convention .1 for the "left" router and .2 for the "right" router on each link, matching the router number in the node name.

docs.title: Device Summary
docs.text: Device Role Loopback R1 Router 10.0.0.1/32 R2 Router 10.0.0.2/32

docs.title: Link Summary
docs.text: Link R1 Interface R1 Address R2 Interface R2 Address R1 — R2 ge-0/0/0 10.1.12.1/30 ge-0/0/0 10.1.12.2/30

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
This is the same two-router project from Session 1. Open JNCIS-SP-Session1 in GNS3 (or create a new project JNCIS-SP-Session2 with the same topology). The link between R1 and R2 on ge-0/0/0 was already drawn in Session 1.

The loopback addresses (10.0.0.X/32) are used throughout this series as stable router identifiers — they remain reachable even if a physical link goes down. OSPF, BGP, and MPLS all reference loopbacks as router IDs.

docs.title: Interface Commands
docs.text: Command Description set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet address X.X.X.X/N Assign IPv4 address to physical interface set interfaces lo0 unit 0 family inet address X.X.X.X/32 Assign loopback address delete interfaces ge-0/0/0 disable Re-enable an admin-down interface set interfaces ge-0/0/0 disable Admin-down an interface show interfaces terse All interfaces — one line each show interfaces ge-0/0/0 Full detail for one interface show interfaces ge-0/0/0 detail Counters and error statistics

docs.title: Routing Commands
docs.text: Command Description set routing-options static route X.X.X.X/N next-hop X.X.X.X Add static route delete routing-options static route X.X.X.X/N Remove static route set routing-options router-id X.X.X.X Set router ID (used by OSPF/BGP) show route Full routing table show route terse Compact routing table show route detail Full detail including next-hop and protocol show route X.X.X.X/N exact Lookup an exact prefix show route X.X.X.X/N longer All more-specific routes within a prefix show route protocol static Only static routes show route protocol direct Only connected routes show route forwarding-table Forwarding table (FIB)

docs.title: Junos Route Preferences (Administrative Distance)
docs.text: Protocol Preference Cisco Equivalent (AD) Direct 0 0 (connected) Local 0 0 (connected) Static 5 1 OSPF internal 10 110 IS-IS Level 1 15 115 IS-IS Level 2 18 115 OSPF external 150 110 (E2) BGP 170 20 (eBGP), 200 (iBGP)

docs.title: Session 2 Wrap-Up
docs.text:
Static routing covers two-router scenarios but does not scale. Every static route must be manually added to every router. When a link fails, static routes to destinations behind that link turn black-hole unless you add BFD or track next-hop reachability.

Session 3 replaces static routes with OSPF, which discovers topology automatically and converges when links fail.

docs.title: Interface Shows up/down
docs.text:
Symptom: show interfaces terse shows ge-0/0/0 up down — admin up, link down.

Cause / Fix: 1. The GNS3 link is not drawn or the remote node is not running — check the canvas 2. The remote node's interface has no cable — right-click the node in GNS3 > Show interfaces 3. The remote node is still booting — wait for the root@ prompt

docs.title: Ping to Remote Interface Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: ping 10.1.12.2 returns Request timeout.

Cause / Fix: 1. Run show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse — must be up up 2. Confirm the correct IP address is assigned: show interfaces ge-0/0/0.0 3. Check the remote router has the other /30 address (10.1.12.2) on its ge-0/0/0.0 4. Confirm both routers are in the same /30 subnet — a /30 has only 2 usable hosts

docs.title: Static Route Not Appearing in show route
docs.text:
Symptom: You configured the static route but it does not appear.

Cause / Fix: 1. Did you commit? Run show | compare — if your route shows as +, you did not commit yet 2. Check the next-hop is reachable: the next-hop must be within a directly connected subnet 3. If the next-hop interface is down, Junos marks the route hidden — run show route hidden to confirm

docs.title: show route hidden
docs.text:
If a static route's next-hop interface goes down, the route becomes hidden (inactive). Use:

show route hidden
show route hidden detail

The detail flag shows why the route is hidden (next-hop dead or interface down).

docs.title: Wrong Interface Configured
docs.text:
Symptom: You configured an address on ge-0/0/1 instead of ge-0/0/0.

Fix:

configure

delete interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family inet address 10.1.12.1/30
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet address 10.1.12.1/30

commit

Always verify with show interfaces terse after committing.

docs.title: Part 0 — Base Configuration
docs.text: Apply this base configuration to both routers. It sets the hostname and root password if you are starting from a fresh vMX boot.

docs.title: CLI Setup
docs.text:
Before pasting any configuration, run these two commands at the operational prompt (>):

set cli screen-length 0
set cli complete-on-space off

Re-run these at the start of every console session — they do not persist across reconnects.

Pasting large config blocks

Use load set terminal in config mode instead of pasting directly at the # prompt. Junos buffers all input until you press Ctrl+D, then processes every set command at once — eliminating paste-rate issues with console terminals.

configure
load set terminal
set system host-name R1
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 ...
^D
commit

docs.title: R1 — Base Config
docs.text:
configure

set system host-name R1
set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123

commit

docs.title: R2 — Base Config
docs.text:
configure

set system host-name R2
set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123

commit

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
On each router, confirm the hostname appears in the prompt:

R1>
R2>

If you completed Session 1 and saved your project, the hostnames are already set. Verify with:

show version | match Hostname

Expected:

Hostname: R1

docs.title: Part 1 — Physical Interface Addressing
docs.text:
Junos interface configuration lives at [edit interfaces]. Each physical interface has one or more units (logical sub-interfaces). For simple IPv4 routing, you use unit 0 with family inet.

Interfaces are up by default

Unlike Cisco IOS — where interfaces start shutdown — Junos interfaces are enabled by default. You do not need a no shutdown equivalent. If an interface is admin-down, you will see disable in its config; remove it with delete interfaces ge-0/0/X disable.

docs.title: R1 — Configure ge-0/0/0
docs.text:
R1> configure

[edit]
R1# set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet address 10.1.12.1/30

[edit]
R1# show | compare
[edit interfaces ge-0/0/0]
+   unit 0 {
+       family inet {
+           address 10.1.12.1/30;
+       }
+   }

[edit]
R1# commit
commit complete

docs.title: R2 — Configure ge-0/0/0
docs.text:
R2> configure

[edit]
R2# set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 0 family inet address 10.1.12.2/30

[edit]
R2# commit
commit complete

docs.title: Verify Link-Layer Connectivity
docs.text:
From R1, ping R2's interface address:

R1> ping 10.1.12.2 count 3
PING 10.1.12.2 (10.1.12.2): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 10.1.12.2: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=1.234 ms
64 bytes from 10.1.12.2: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=0.987 ms
64 bytes from 10.1.12.2: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=1.102 ms

If ping fails

  1. Run show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse on both routers — both should show up up in the first two columns (physical state, protocol state)
  2. Check the GNS3 link is green between R1 and R2
  3. Confirm you configured ge-0/0/0 (not ge-0/0/1) on both ends

docs.title: Understanding show interfaces terse
docs.text:
R1> show interfaces terse
Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
ge-0/0/0                up    up
ge-0/0/0.0              up    up   inet     10.1.12.1/30
ge-0/0/1                up    down
ge-0/0/2                up    down
ge-0/0/3                up    down
lo0                     up    up
lo0.0                   up    up   inet     127.0.0.1/8
  • Column 1 (Admin): up = not disabled
  • Column 2 (Link): up = physical carrier detected (link partner present)
  • ge-0/0/0.0 is the logical unit you configured — shows the IP address

ge-0/0/1 through ge-0/0/3 are up/down because no cable is connected to those ports.

docs.title: Configure Loopback Interfaces
docs.text:
Loopbacks in Junos use lo0 (the only loopback device). Each router has a single lo0 with one or more units.

Why /32 on the loopback?

A /32 means "this address belongs only to this router." Routing protocols advertise loopbacks as /32 host routes, making them reachable from anywhere in the network. They do not depend on any physical link being up.

docs.title: R1 — Loopback
docs.text:
R1> configure

[edit]
R1# set interfaces lo0 unit 0 family inet address 10.0.0.1/32

[edit]
R1# commit
commit complete

docs.title: R2 — Loopback
docs.text:
R2> configure

[edit]
R2# set interfaces lo0 unit 0 family inet address 10.0.0.2/32

[edit]
R2# commit
commit complete

Verify loopbacks appear in show interfaces terse:

R1> show interfaces lo0 terse
Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
lo0                     up    up
lo0.0                   up    up   inet     10.0.0.1/32
                                            127.0.0.1/8

docs.title: Configure Static Routes
docs.text:
Static routes in Junos live under [edit routing-options static]. The syntax is:

set routing-options static route <destination> next-hop <gateway>

docs.title: R1 — Static Route to R2 Loopback
docs.text:
R1> configure

[edit]
R1# set routing-options static route 10.0.0.2/32 next-hop 10.1.12.2

[edit]
R1# show | compare
[edit routing-options]
+  static {
+      route 10.0.0.2/32 next-hop 10.1.12.2;
+  }

[edit]
R1# commit
commit complete

docs.title: R2 — Static Route to R1 Loopback
docs.text:
R2> configure

[edit]
R2# set routing-options static route 10.0.0.1/32 next-hop 10.1.12.1

[edit]
R2# commit
commit complete

docs.title: Verify the Routing Table
docs.text:
R1> show route

inet.0: 4 destinations, 4 routes (4 active, 0 holddown, 0 hidden)
+ = Active Route, - = Last Active, * = Both

10.0.0.1/32        *[Direct/0] 00:03:21
                    > via lo0.0
10.0.0.2/32        *[Static/5] 00:00:42
                    > to 10.1.12.2 via ge-0/0/0.0
10.1.12.0/30       *[Direct/0] 00:05:11
                    > via ge-0/0/0.0
10.1.12.1/30       *[Local/0] 00:05:11
                      Local via ge-0/0/0.0

Route types: - Direct/0 — directly connected network (preference 0) - Local/0 — local IP address on an interface - Static/5 — static route (preference 5)

Junos route preference

Junos uses preference instead of Cisco's administrative distance. Lower = preferred. Static routes have preference 5 (vs Cisco's 1). OSPF internal = 10, BGP = 170.

docs.title: Verify Loopback-to-Loopback Ping
docs.text:
R1> ping 10.0.0.2 count 3
PING 10.0.0.2 (10.0.0.2): 56 data bytes
64 bytes from 10.0.0.2: icmp_seq=0 ttl=64 time=1.345 ms
64 bytes from 10.0.0.2: icmp_seq=1 ttl=64 time=1.123 ms
64 bytes from 10.0.0.2: icmp_seq=2 ttl=64 time=0.998 ms

This confirms the static route is installed and traffic can reach R2's loopback.

docs.title: Default Routes
docs.text: A default route (0.0.0.0/0) matches any destination not in the routing table. In a service provider network, default routes are typically used on customer edge (CE) routers pointing toward the provider.

docs.title: R2 — Add a Default Route Pointing to R1
docs.text:
R2> configure

[edit]
R2# set routing-options static route 0.0.0.0/0 next-hop 10.1.12.1

[edit]
R2# commit
commit complete

Verify it appears in the routing table:

R2> show route 0.0.0.0/0

inet.0: 5 destinations, 5 routes (5 active, 0 holddown, 0 hidden)
+ = Active Route, - = Last Active, * = Both

0.0.0.0/0          *[Static/5] 00:00:15
                    > to 10.1.12.1 via ge-0/0/0.0

Remove the default route when done — in production SP networks, default routes are distributed via BGP, not configured statically on transit routers:

R2> configure

[edit]
R2# delete routing-options static route 0.0.0.0/0

[edit]
R2# commit
commit complete

docs.title: Qualified Next-Hops (Floating Static Routes)
docs.text:
Junos supports floating static routes — a backup route with a higher preference that activates only when the primary route disappears.

R1> configure

[edit]
R1# set routing-options static route 10.0.0.2/32 next-hop 10.1.12.2
R1# set routing-options static route 10.0.0.2/32 qualified-next-hop 10.1.12.2 preference 10

[edit]
R1# commit
commit complete

Floating static routes in Junos

The qualified-next-hop with a higher preference value serves as the backup. If the primary next-hop becomes unreachable, the qualified next-hop activates. This pattern is less common once dynamic routing protocols are in place.

docs.title: Examine the Full Routing Table
docs.text:
R1> show route detail

The detail flag shows next-hop type, preference, metric, and age for each route. Use show route <prefix> detail to inspect a specific prefix.

R1> show route 10.0.0.2/32 detail

inet.0: 4 destinations, 4 routes (4 active, 0 holddown, 0 hidden)
10.0.0.2/32 (1 entry, 1 announced)
        *Static Preference: 5
                Next hop: 10.1.12.2 via ge-0/0/0.0, selected
                State: <Active Int>
                Age: 2:34
                Task: RT
                Announcement bits (1): 0-KRT
                AS path: I

docs.title: Useful Route Filtering
docs.text: Command What it shows show route protocol static Only static routes show route protocol direct Only directly connected routes show route 10.0.0.0/8 longer All routes within 10.0.0.0/8 show route 10.0.0.2/32 exact Only the exact prefix show route terse Compact one-line format per route

docs.title: Interfaces
docs.text:
• R1> show interfaces terse — ge-0/0/0.0 shows up up inet 10.1.12.1/30
  • R2> show interfaces terse — ge-0/0/0.0 shows up up inet 10.1.12.2/30
  • R1> show interfaces terse — lo0.0 shows up up inet 10.0.0.1/32
  • R2> show interfaces terse — lo0.0 shows up up inet 10.0.0.2/32

docs.title: Routing Table
docs.text:
• R1> show route — 4 routes: 10.0.0.1/32 (Direct), 10.0.0.2/32 (Static), 10.1.12.0/30 (Direct), 10.1.12.1/30 (Local)
  • R2> show route — 4 routes: 10.0.0.2/32 (Direct), 10.0.0.1/32 (Static), 10.1.12.0/30 (Direct), 10.1.12.2/30 (Local)

docs.title: Connectivity
docs.text:
• R1> ping 10.1.12.2 count 3 — 3/3 packets received
  • R2> ping 10.1.12.1 count 3 — 3/3 packets received
  • R1> ping 10.0.0.2 count 3 — 3/3 packets received (via static route)
  • R2> ping 10.0.0.1 count 3 — 3/3 packets received (via static route)

docs.title: Quick Verification Commands
docs.text:
show interfaces terse
show route
show route protocol static
show route 10.0.0.2/32 detail

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session you will be able to:

  • Change the vMX network-services mode to enhanced-ethernet and reboot
  • Define bridge domains with VLAN IDs in Junos
  • Configure access ports (untagged) for PC endpoints
  • Configure trunk ports (tagged, multiple VLANs) between two vMX switches
  • Verify MAC address learning in a bridge domain
  • Add IRB interfaces for inter-VLAN routing

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Sessions 1 and 2 complete — you can boot vMX nodes and configure interfaces
  • GNS3 project with two vMX nodes and four VPCS nodes available

docs.title: Topology Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
  PC1([PC1<br/>192.168.10.1<br/>VLAN 10]) --- SW1([SW1<br/>vMX])
  PC2([PC2<br/>192.168.11.1<br/>VLAN 11]) --- SW1
  SW1 ---|trunk VLAN 10+11| SW2([SW2<br/>vMX])
  SW2 --- PC3([PC3<br/>192.168.10.2<br/>VLAN 10])
  SW2 --- PC4([PC4<br/>192.168.11.2<br/>VLAN 11])

Two vMX routers act as Layer 2 switches using Junos bridge domains. Each switch has one PC in VLAN 10 and one in VLAN 11. The trunk carries both VLANs between the switches — PC1 and PC3 share VLAN 10 and can communicate across the trunk; PC2 and PC4 share VLAN 11. VLAN 10 and VLAN 11 are isolated from each other at Layer 2.

docs.title: Session Parts
docs.text:
Part Topic Part 0 Base config — enable enhanced-ethernet mode Part 1 Bridge domains and access ports Part 2 Trunk configuration Part 3 IRB for inter-VLAN routing Verification Checklist

VCP-only limitation

This session uses vMX in VCP-only mode (no separate VFP). Bridge domain traffic is forwarded by the RE in software. The configuration and verification commands all work correctly — packet forwarding functions but at lower throughput than a production MX with a VFP.

docs.title: VLAN Assignment
docs.text: VLAN ID Name Subnet Purpose 10 VLAN10 192.168.10.0/24 PC1 and PC3 segment 11 VLAN11 192.168.11.0/24 PC2 and PC4 segment

docs.title: IRB Addresses (Part 3)
docs.text: Interface Device IP Address VLAN irb.10 SW1 192.168.10.254/24 10 irb.11 SW1 192.168.11.254/24 11

docs.title: Interface Roles
docs.text:
Device Interface Adapter Role SW1 ge-0/0/0 2 Trunk to SW2 (VLANs 10, 11) SW1 ge-0/0/1 3 Access port, VLAN 10 → PC1 SW1 ge-0/0/2 4 Access port, VLAN 11 → PC2 SW2 ge-0/0/0 2 Trunk to SW1 (VLANs 10, 11) SW2 ge-0/0/1 3 Access port, VLAN 10 → PC3 SW2 ge-0/0/2 4 Access port, VLAN 11 → PC4

No routing addresses on switch interfaces

In Parts 1 and 2 the vMX interfaces carry only bridged traffic — no family inet addresses are needed. IP addresses are added only in Part 3 via IRB interfaces.

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
  PC1([PC1<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.10.1/24]) --- SW1
  PC2([PC2<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.11.1/24]) --- SW1
  SW1([SW1<br/>vMX]) ---|ge-0/0/0 trunk| SW2([SW2<br/>vMX])
  SW2 --- PC3([PC3<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.10.2/24])
  SW2 --- PC4([PC4<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.11.2/24])

docs.title: Device Summary
docs.text: Device Role Image GNS3 Node SW1 Layer 2 switch (bridge domains) vMX 14.1R4.8 vMX-14.1 template SW2 Layer 2 switch (bridge domains) vMX 14.1R4.8 vMX-14.1 template PC1 End host, VLAN 10 (on SW1) VPCS Built-in GNS3 PC2 End host, VLAN 11 (on SW1) VPCS Built-in GNS3 PC3 End host, VLAN 10 (on SW2) VPCS Built-in GNS3 PC4 End host, VLAN 11 (on SW2) VPCS Built-in GNS3

docs.title: Link Summary
docs.text: Link SW Interface PC/SW Interface Type SW1 — SW2 ge-0/0/0 (Adapter 2) ge-0/0/0 (Adapter 2) 802.1Q trunk, VLANs 10 + 11 SW1 — PC1 ge-0/0/1 (Adapter 3) eth0 Access, VLAN 10 (untagged) SW1 — PC2 ge-0/0/2 (Adapter 4) eth0 Access, VLAN 11 (untagged) SW2 — PC3 ge-0/0/1 (Adapter 3) eth0 Access, VLAN 10 (untagged) SW2 — PC4 ge-0/0/2 (Adapter 4) eth0 Access, VLAN 11 (untagged)

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• PC1 and PC3 are both in VLAN 10 — they can communicate at Layer 2 across the trunk without routing
  • PC2 and PC4 are both in VLAN 11 — same behaviour
  • VLAN 10 and VLAN 11 are isolated from each other; cross-VLAN communication requires IRB (Part 3)
  • SW1 holds the IRB interfaces that act as the default gateway for both VLANs

docs.title: Network Services
docs.text: Command Description show chassis network-services Show current mode (Enhanced-IP or Enhanced-Ethernet) set chassis network-services enhanced-ethernet Enable bridge domain support (requires reboot)

docs.title: Bridge Domain Commands
docs.text: Command Description show bridge domain List all bridge domains, interface counts, IRB status show bridge mac-table Show learned MAC addresses per bridge domain show bridge mac-table bridge-domain VLAN10 MAC table for a specific domain show l2-learning global-information Global L2 learning state clear bridge mac-table Flush all learned MAC addresses

docs.title: Interface Commands
docs.text: Command Description show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse Physical + subunits, admin/link state show interfaces irb terse All IRB interfaces and their IPs show interfaces ge-0/0/0 detail Full counters, encapsulation type

docs.title: Bridge domain
docs.text:
[edit bridge-domains VLAN10]
  vlan-id 10;
  description "...";
  interface ge-0/0/1.0;
  interface ge-0/0/0.10;
  routing-interface irb.10;   /* Part 3 only */

docs.title: Access port
docs.text:
[edit interfaces ge-0/0/1]
  encapsulation ethernet-bridge;
  unit 0 {
    family bridge {
      interface-mode access;
      vlan-id 10;
    }
  }

docs.title: Trunk port
docs.text:
[edit interfaces ge-0/0/0]
  flexible-vlan-tagging;
  encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services;
  unit 10 {
    encapsulation vlan-bridge;
    vlan-id 10;
  }
  unit 11 {
    encapsulation vlan-bridge;
    vlan-id 11;
  }

docs.title: IRB interface
docs.text:
[edit interfaces irb]
  unit 10 {
    family inet {
      address 192.168.10.254/24;
    }
  }

docs.title: Wrap-Up
docs.text:
After this session, the vMX is configured as a full Layer 2 switch with:

  • Two isolated broadcast domains (VLAN 10 and VLAN 11)
  • 802.1Q trunking between two switches
  • IRB interfaces providing Layer 3 inter-VLAN routing

Session 4 returns to Layer 3 with OSPF — the SP core topology begins there and remains constant through Session 8.

docs.title: Bridge Domain Not Appearing After Commit
docs.text:
Symptom: show bridge domain returns nothing or "no bridge domains found" after configuring.

Cause: The vMX is still in enhanced-ip mode. Bridge domains only function in enhanced-ethernet mode.

Fix: 1. Check the current mode: show chassis network-services 2. If it shows Enhanced-IP, the mode change did not take effect 3. Enter config mode, re-apply set chassis network-services enhanced-ethernet, commit, and reboot

docs.title: Interface Shows Proto: - Instead of Bridge
docs.text:
Symptom: show interfaces ge-0/0/1 terse shows ge-0/0/1.0 up up - with no protocol.

Cause: family bridge was not configured on the unit, or the interface was not added to a bridge domain.

Fix: 

configure
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 encapsulation ethernet-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge interface-mode access
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge vlan-id 10
set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/1.0
commit

docs.title: Trunk Subunits Not Coming Up
docs.text:
Symptom: ge-0/0/0.10 and ge-0/0/0.11 show down down after configuring the trunk.

Cause / Fix:

  1. Confirm flexible-vlan-tagging and encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services are set on the physical interface (ge-0/0/0), not on a unit
  2. Confirm the physical link is up: show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse — the parent line should show up up
  3. Confirm the GNS3 link is connected on Adapter 2 (ge-0/0/0) on both SW1 and SW2
  4. Confirm both ends have matching VLAN IDs configured on the trunk subunits

docs.title: PC Ping to Gateway Fails
docs.text:
Symptom: PC1 cannot ping 192.168.10.254 after Part 3.

Cause / Fix:

  1. Confirm IRB interface is up: show interfaces irb terse — irb.10 must show up up inet
  2. Confirm set bridge-domains VLAN10 routing-interface irb.10 was committed
  3. Confirm PC1's access port (ge-0/0/1.0) is in the VLAN10 bridge domain: show bridge domain VLAN10
  4. Confirm PC1's IP and gateway are set correctly from the VPCS console: show ip

docs.title: Inter-VLAN Ping Fails (PC1 → PC2)
docs.text:
Symptom: PC1 can ping its gateway (192.168.10.254) but cannot reach PC2 (192.168.11.1).

Cause / Fix:

  1. Confirm irb.11 is up and has address 192.168.11.254/24
  2. Confirm PC2's gateway is set to 192.168.11.254 (not 192.168.10.254)
  3. Confirm SW1 has both irb.10 and irb.11 bound to their bridge domains
  4. Check the routing table: show route — should show both 192.168.10.0/24 and 192.168.11.0/24

docs.title: show bridge mac-table is Empty
docs.text:
Symptom: No MAC addresses appear even after pinging.

Cause: In VCP-only mode, software forwarding may not populate the MAC table in all code paths.

Impact: Low — routing and bridging may still work. The MAC table is a diagnostic aid, not a requirement for forwarding in this lab.

Workaround: Generate traffic (several pings), then check again. If still empty, proceed — verify connectivity directly with pings rather than relying on the MAC table.

docs.title: Part 0 — Enable Enhanced-Ethernet Mode
docs.text:
vMX supports bridge domains only in enhanced-ethernet network-services mode. The default after initial setup is enhanced-ip. You must change this mode and reboot before any bridging configuration will take effect.

Reboot required

Changing network-services is a chassis-level change. The router must be rebooted immediately after committing — there is no way to apply it live. This is expected behaviour.

docs.title: CLI Setup
docs.text:
Before pasting any configuration, run these two commands at the operational prompt (>) on each switch:

set cli screen-length 0
set cli complete-on-space off

Re-run these at the start of every console session — they do not persist across reconnects.

Pasting large config blocks

Use load set terminal in config mode instead of pasting directly at the # prompt. Junos buffers all input until you press Ctrl+D, then processes every set command at once — eliminating paste-rate issues with console terminals.

configure
load set terminal
set system host-name SW1
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 ...
^D
commit

docs.title: Step 1: Set hostnames
docs.text:
On SW1:

configure
set system host-name SW1
set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123
commit

On SW2:

configure
set system host-name SW2
set system root-authentication plain-text-password
New password: lab123
Retype new password: lab123
commit

docs.title: Step 2: Change network-services mode
docs.text:
Apply this on both SW1 and SW2:

configure
set chassis network-services enhanced-ethernet
commit

Expected output:

commit complete

docs.title: Step 3: Reboot
docs.text:
On each router immediately after commit:

request system reboot

Confirm with yes. Wait 3–5 minutes for the router to come back up.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify the mode change
docs.text:
After reboot, confirm the mode:

show chassis network-services

Expected:

Network Services Mode: Enhanced-Ethernet

If you see Enhanced-IP

The mode did not apply. Re-enter configuration mode, re-apply set chassis network-services enhanced-ethernet, commit, and reboot again. Confirm you typed the value exactly — enhanced-ethernet not enhanced_ethernet.

docs.title: Part 1 — Bridge Domains and Access Ports
docs.text: A bridge domain in Junos is the equivalent of a VLAN on a traditional switch. It defines a Layer 2 forwarding domain identified by a VLAN ID. Interfaces added to a bridge domain forward traffic at Layer 2 within that domain only.

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text: Term Junos equivalent Cisco equivalent VLAN / broadcast domain Bridge domain VLAN Access port (untagged) interface-mode access under family bridge switchport mode access Trunk port (tagged) flexible-vlan-tagging + encapsulation vlan-bridge switchport mode trunk SVI / VLAN interface IRB (Integrated Routing and Bridging) interface vlan X

docs.title: Step 1: Add VPCS nodes in GNS3
docs.text:
1. Drag four VPCS nodes onto the canvas and label them PC1, PC2, PC3, PC4
  2. Connect PC1 to SW1 Adapter 3 (ge-0/0/1)
  3. Connect PC2 to SW1 Adapter 4 (ge-0/0/2)
  4. Connect PC3 to SW2 Adapter 3 (ge-0/0/1)
  5. Connect PC4 to SW2 Adapter 4 (ge-0/0/2)
  6. Do not connect the trunk yet — that is Part 2

docs.title: Step 2: Define bridge domains on SW1
docs.text:
configure

set bridge-domains VLAN10 vlan-id 10
set bridge-domains VLAN10 description "VLAN 10 segment"
set bridge-domains VLAN11 vlan-id 11
set bridge-domains VLAN11 description "VLAN 11 segment"

commit

Repeat on SW2 — both switches must define the same bridge domains for the trunk in Part 2 to work.

docs.title: Step 3: Configure access ports on SW1
docs.text:
VLAN 10 access port for PC1 (ge-0/0/1):

configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/1 encapsulation ethernet-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge interface-mode access
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge vlan-id 10

commit

VLAN 11 access port for PC2 (ge-0/0/2):

configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/2 encapsulation ethernet-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/2 unit 0 family bridge interface-mode access
set interfaces ge-0/0/2 unit 0 family bridge vlan-id 11

commit

No explicit bridge-domain interface statement for access ports

When interface-mode access is set with a vlan-id, Junos automatically associates the interface with the bridge domain that matches that VLAN ID. Adding set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/1.0 explicitly will cause a commit error. The explicit interface statement is only required for trunk subunits (Part 2).

docs.title: Step 4: Configure access ports on SW2
docs.text:
VLAN 10 access port for PC3 (ge-0/0/1):

configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/1 encapsulation ethernet-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge interface-mode access
set interfaces ge-0/0/1 unit 0 family bridge vlan-id 10

commit

VLAN 11 access port for PC4 (ge-0/0/2):

configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/2 encapsulation ethernet-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/2 unit 0 family bridge interface-mode access
set interfaces ge-0/0/2 unit 0 family bridge vlan-id 11

commit

docs.title: Step 5: Assign IPs to VPCS nodes
docs.text:
Open each VPCS console (right-click > Console in GNS3) and apply:

PC1 (SW1, VLAN 10): 

ip 192.168.10.1 192.168.10.254 24

PC2 (SW1, VLAN 11): 

ip 192.168.11.1 192.168.11.254 24

PC3 (SW2, VLAN 10): 

ip 192.168.10.2 192.168.10.254 24

PC4 (SW2, VLAN 11): 

ip 192.168.11.2 192.168.11.254 24

The gateway addresses (.254) will not resolve until Part 3 when the IRB interface is added.

docs.title: Verify
docs.text:
show bridge domain

Expected on SW1:

Routing instance        Bridge domain            VLAN ID     Interfaces
default-switch          VLAN10                   10
                                                     ge-0/0/1.0
default-switch          VLAN11                   11
                                                     ge-0/0/2.0

Each bridge domain shows its local access port. The trunk has not been added yet.

show interfaces ge-0/0/1 terse
show interfaces ge-0/0/2 terse

Expected on SW1:

Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
ge-0/0/1                up    up
ge-0/0/1.0              up    up   Bridge
ge-0/0/2                up    up
ge-0/0/2.0              up    up   Bridge

At this stage, no cross-switch communication is possible — the trunk is not yet configured.

docs.title: Part 2 — Trunk Configuration
docs.text: A trunk carries multiple VLANs over a single link using 802.1Q tagging. In Junos, a trunk port is built by enabling flexible-vlan-tagging on the physical interface and creating a logical subunit per VLAN with encapsulation vlan-bridge.

docs.title: Step 1: Draw the trunk link in GNS3
docs.text:
1. Click Add a link
  2. Click SW1 → select Adapter 2 (ge-0/0/0)
  3. Click SW2 → select Adapter 2 (ge-0/0/0)

docs.title: Step 2: Configure the trunk on SW1
docs.text:
configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 flexible-vlan-tagging
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 10 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 10 vlan-id 10

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 11 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 11 vlan-id 11

set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/0.10
set bridge-domains VLAN11 interface ge-0/0/0.11

commit

docs.title: Step 3: Configure the trunk on SW2
docs.text:
Apply the same trunk config on SW2 (both ends must match):

configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 flexible-vlan-tagging
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 10 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 10 vlan-id 10

set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 11 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/0 unit 11 vlan-id 11

set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/0.10
set bridge-domains VLAN11 interface ge-0/0/0.11

commit

docs.title: Step 4: Verify the trunk is up
docs.text:
show interfaces ge-0/0/0 terse

Expected on both switches:

Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
ge-0/0/0                up    up
ge-0/0/0.10             up    up   Bridge
ge-0/0/0.11             up    up   Bridge

show bridge domain

Expected on SW1 (and mirrored on SW2):

Routing instance        Bridge domain            VLAN ID     Interfaces
default-switch          VLAN10                   10
                                                     ge-0/0/0.10
                                                     ge-0/0/1.0
default-switch          VLAN11                   11
                                                     ge-0/0/0.11
                                                     ge-0/0/2.0

Each bridge domain now shows two interfaces: the local access port and the trunk subunit.

docs.title: Step 5: Test same-VLAN communication across the trunk
docs.text:
From PC1 (192.168.10.1), ping PC3 (192.168.10.2) — both are in VLAN 10:

ping 192.168.10.2

Expected: replies from PC3. The trunk is carrying VLAN 10 between the switches.

From PC2 (192.168.11.1), ping PC4 (192.168.11.2) — both are in VLAN 11:

ping 192.168.11.2

Expected: replies from PC4.

docs.title: Step 6: Confirm VLAN isolation
docs.text:
From PC1 (VLAN 10), attempt to ping PC2 (VLAN 11, 192.168.11.1):

ping 192.168.11.1

Expected: no reply. PC1 and PC2 are in different bridge domains — they cannot communicate at Layer 2 even though they are connected to the same physical switch.

This is the core purpose of VLANs: traffic separation on shared infrastructure.

Cross-VLAN communication requires a Layer 3 device. That is added in Part 3.

MAC learning

After the pings succeed, you can view learned MAC addresses per VLAN: 

show bridge mac-table

docs.title: Part 3 — IRB for Inter-VLAN Routing
docs.text: An IRB (Integrated Routing and Bridging) interface is a logical Layer 3 interface attached to a bridge domain. It gives the vMX a routable IP address within a VLAN — equivalent to a Cisco SVI (interface Vlan10). With IRB interfaces on both VLANs, SW1 acts as the default gateway and routes traffic between VLAN 10 and VLAN 11.

docs.title: Step 1: Add IRB interfaces to SW1
docs.text:
configure

set interfaces irb unit 10 family inet address 192.168.10.254/24
set interfaces irb unit 11 family inet address 192.168.11.254/24

set bridge-domains VLAN10 routing-interface irb.10
set bridge-domains VLAN11 routing-interface irb.11

commit

docs.title: Step 2: Verify IRB interfaces are up
docs.text:
show interfaces irb terse

Expected:

Interface               Admin Link Proto    Local                 Remote
irb.10                  up    up   inet     192.168.10.254/24
irb.11                  up    up   inet     192.168.11.254/24

show bridge domain

Expected (vMX 14.1 does not display an IRB column — verify IRB with show interfaces irb terse instead):

Routing instance        Bridge domain            VLAN ID     Interfaces
default-switch          VLAN10                   10
                                                     ge-0/0/0.10
                                                     ge-0/0/1.0
default-switch          VLAN11                   11
                                                     ge-0/0/0.11
                                                     ge-0/0/2.0

docs.title: Step 3: Test gateway reachability from each PC
docs.text:
From PC1: 

ping 192.168.10.254

From PC2: 

ping 192.168.11.254

From PC3: 

ping 192.168.10.254

From PC4: 

ping 192.168.11.254

All four should receive replies from SW1's IRB interfaces.

docs.title: Step 4: Test inter-VLAN routing
docs.text:
From PC1 (VLAN 10), ping PC2 (VLAN 11):

ping 192.168.11.1

From PC3 (VLAN 10), ping PC4 (VLAN 11):

ping 192.168.11.2

Expected: replies in both cases. SW1 routes the packets between bridge domains through its IRB interfaces.

docs.title: Step 5: Check the routing table on SW1
docs.text:
show route

Expected:

inet.0: 2 destinations, 2 routes
  192.168.10.0/24     *[Direct/0] ...
  192.168.11.0/24     *[Direct/0] ...

docs.title: How it works
docs.text:
PC1 (192.168.10.1) → SW1 ge-0/0/1.0 (bridge, VLAN10)
                   → SW1 irb.10 (L3 gateway 192.168.10.254)
                   → SW1 irb.11 (L3 gateway 192.168.11.254)
                   → SW1 ge-0/0/2.0 (bridge, VLAN11)
                   → PC2 (192.168.11.1)

PC3 (192.168.10.2) → SW2 ge-0/0/1.0 (bridge, VLAN10)
                   → SW2 ge-0/0/0.10 (trunk, VLAN10)
                   → SW1 ge-0/0/0.10 (trunk, VLAN10)
                   → SW1 irb.10 → irb.11
                   → SW1 ge-0/0/0.11 (trunk, VLAN11)
                   → SW2 ge-0/0/0.11 (trunk, VLAN11)
                   → SW2 ge-0/0/2.0 (bridge, VLAN11)
                   → PC4 (192.168.11.2)

All inter-VLAN routing passes through SW1's IRB interfaces, regardless of which switch the source and destination PCs are connected to.

docs.title: Session 3 — Verification Checklist
docs.text: Complete these checks before moving to Session 4.

docs.title: Platform
docs.text:
• Both SW1 and SW2 show Network Services Mode: Enhanced-Ethernet (show chassis network-services)
  • Both switches have VLAN10 and VLAN11 bridge domains defined (show bridge domain)

docs.title: Access Ports
docs.text:
• SW1 ge-0/0/1.0 is in VLAN10 bridge domain and shows up up Bridge
  • SW1 ge-0/0/2.0 is in VLAN11 bridge domain and shows up up Bridge
  • SW2 ge-0/0/1.0 is in VLAN10 bridge domain and shows up up Bridge
  • SW2 ge-0/0/2.0 is in VLAN11 bridge domain and shows up up Bridge
  • PC1 has IP 192.168.10.1/24 with gateway 192.168.10.254
  • PC2 has IP 192.168.11.1/24 with gateway 192.168.11.254
  • PC3 has IP 192.168.10.2/24 with gateway 192.168.10.254
  • PC4 has IP 192.168.11.2/24 with gateway 192.168.11.254

docs.title: Trunk
docs.text:
• SW1 and SW2 ge-0/0/0 trunk is up with subunits .10 and .11 showing up up Bridge
  • Both VLAN10 and VLAN11 bridge domains show 2 interfaces on each switch (access + trunk)
  • PC1 (192.168.10.1) can ping PC3 (192.168.10.2) — same VLAN across trunk
  • PC2 (192.168.11.1) can ping PC4 (192.168.11.2) — same VLAN across trunk
  • PC1 cannot ping PC2 (different VLANs, no routing yet)

docs.title: IRB / Inter-VLAN Routing
docs.text:
• SW1 irb.10 shows 192.168.10.254/24 and is up up
  • SW1 irb.11 shows 192.168.11.254/24 and is up up
  • All four PCs can ping their default gateway (192.168.10.254 or 192.168.11.254)
  • PC1 can ping PC2 (192.168.11.1) — inter-VLAN routing works
  • PC3 can ping PC4 (192.168.11.2) — inter-VLAN routing works across trunk

docs.title: Quick Commands
docs.text:
show chassis network-services
Expected: Network Services Mode: Enhanced-Ethernet

show bridge domain
Expected: VLAN10 and VLAN11 each listing ge-0/0/0.1x (trunk) and ge-0/0/1.0 or ge-0/0/2.0 (access port). vMX 14.1 does not display an IRB column — IRB is verified separately with show interfaces irb terse

show bridge mac-table
Expected: MAC addresses for all four PCs in their respective bridge domains after traffic is generated

show interfaces irb terse
Expected: irb.10 and irb.11 both up up inet

show route
Expected: 192.168.10.0/24 and 192.168.11.0/24 as Direct routes via irb.10 and irb.11

docs.title: Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this session you will be able to:

  • Explain why STP is required when redundant Layer 2 paths exist
  • Add a second trunk link to create a physical loop
  • Enable RSTP on bridge domains and observe the root bridge election
  • Identify port roles: Root, Designated, and Alternate (blocking)
  • Demonstrate RSTP fast failover by removing the active trunk

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Session 3 complete — both vMX switches running in enhanced-ethernet mode with VLAN 10 and VLAN 11 bridge domains configured
  • Four VPCS PCs connected and passing traffic

docs.title: Why Spanning Tree?
docs.text:
Without STP, a second trunk between two switches creates a loop. Ethernet frames have no TTL — a broadcast sent by PC1 would be forwarded endlessly between SW1 and SW2, consuming all available bandwidth and crashing both switches within seconds.

STP solves this by electing a root bridge and logically blocking all redundant paths, leaving exactly one active path between any two devices. If the active path fails, STP unblocks the redundant path to restore connectivity.

RSTP (Rapid Spanning Tree, IEEE 802.1w) is the modern version — it converges in under one second instead of the 30–50 seconds required by original STP (802.1D).

docs.title: Topology Overview
docs.text:
graph LR
  PC1([PC1<br/>VLAN 10]) --- SW1([SW1<br/>vMX<br/>Root Bridge])
  PC2([PC2<br/>VLAN 11]) --- SW1
  SW1 ===|Trunk 1 ge-0/0/0 — Forwarding| SW2([SW2<br/>vMX])
  SW1 ---|Trunk 2 ge-0/0/3 — Blocked by STP| SW2
  SW2 --- PC3([PC3<br/>VLAN 10])
  SW2 --- PC4([PC4<br/>VLAN 11])

STP blocks one of the two trunk links, preventing the loop while keeping the redundant path available for failover.

docs.title: Session Parts
docs.text:
Part Topic Part 0 Enable RSTP on both switches Part 1 Add second trunk — RSTP blocks the loop Part 2 Port roles and states Part 3 RSTP failover test Verification Checklist

vMX 14.1 STP scope

On vMX 14.1, RSTP is configured globally under [edit protocols rstp] — there is no per-bridge-domain STP stanza. A single RSTP instance covers all bridge domains.

docs.title: IP Addressing
docs.text:
No new IP addresses are introduced in this session. PC addresses and IRB gateway addresses remain identical to Session 3.

Device VLAN IP Address Gateway PC1 10 192.168.10.1/24 192.168.10.254 PC2 11 192.168.11.1/24 192.168.11.254 PC3 10 192.168.10.2/24 192.168.10.254 PC4 11 192.168.11.2/24 192.168.11.254

docs.title: New Interface Roles (Session 3a additions)
docs.text: Device Interface Adapter Role SW1 ge-0/0/3 5 Trunk 2 to SW2 (VLANs 10, 11) SW2 ge-0/0/3 5 Trunk 2 to SW1 (VLANs 10, 11)

docs.title: STP Parameters
docs.text: Parameter SW1 SW2 Bridge priority 4096 (root) 8192 (8k) Hello time 2s (default) 2s (default) Max age 20s (default) 20s (default) Forward delay 15s (default) 15s (default)

docs.title: Diagram
docs.text:
graph LR
  PC1([PC1<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.10.1/24]) --- SW1
  PC2([PC2<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.11.1/24]) --- SW1
  SW1([SW1<br/>vMX]) ===|Trunk 1 ge-0/0/0| SW2([SW2<br/>vMX])
  SW1 ---|Trunk 2 ge-0/0/3| SW2
  SW2 --- PC3([PC3<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.10.2/24])
  SW2 --- PC4([PC4<br/>VPCS<br/>192.168.11.2/24])

docs.title: Device Summary
docs.text: Device Role STP Role SW1 Layer 2 switch Root bridge (bridge-priority 4096) SW2 Layer 2 switch Non-root bridge (bridge-priority 32768) PC1–PC4 End hosts N/A

docs.title: Link Summary
docs.text: Link SW1 Interface SW2 Interface Type STP State Trunk 1 ge-0/0/0 (Adapter 2) ge-0/0/0 (Adapter 2) 802.1Q trunk Forwarding Trunk 2 ge-0/0/3 (Adapter 5) ge-0/0/3 (Adapter 5) 802.1Q trunk Blocked (SW2 alternate) SW1 — PC1 ge-0/0/1 (Adapter 3) — Access VLAN 10 Edge / Forwarding SW1 — PC2 ge-0/0/2 (Adapter 4) — Access VLAN 11 Edge / Forwarding SW2 — PC3 ge-0/0/1 (Adapter 3) — Access VLAN 10 Edge / Forwarding SW2 — PC4 ge-0/0/2 (Adapter 4) — Access VLAN 11 Edge / Forwarding

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• The STP state in the table above reflects the expected state after SW1 is elected root bridge
  • The actual blocked port may be on either switch depending on port costs and MAC addresses — the root bridge's ports are always Designated (forwarding)
  • PC access ports are configured as edge ports in RSTP — they transition immediately to forwarding without going through the listening/learning states

docs.title: STP Show Commands
docs.text: Command Description show spanning-tree bridge Root bridge ID, priority, hello/age/forward timers show spanning-tree interface Port roles and states for all STP-active interfaces show spanning-tree statistics BPDU counters per interface show spanning-tree statistics interface ge-0/0/0.10 BPDU stats for one interface

docs.title: Enable RSTP globally (vMX 14.1)
docs.text:
[edit protocols rstp]
  bridge-priority 4096;   /* SW1 root — lower value = preferred */
  interface ge-0/0/0;     /* Trunk 1 — participates in STP topology */
  interface ge-0/0/3;     /* Trunk 2 — participates in STP topology */
  interface ge-0/0/1 {
    edge;                 /* access port — skip state machine, forward immediately */
  }
  interface ge-0/0/2 {
    edge;
  }

[edit protocols rstp]
  bridge-priority 8192;   /* SW2 non-root — use 8k shorthand in set mode */
  interface ge-0/0/0;
  interface ge-0/0/3;

Note: on vMX 14.1, bridge-priority must be specified explicitly and each trunk interface must be added individually with set protocols rstp interface <name>. protocols rstp is global; there is no per-bridge-domain STP stanza.

docs.title: Mark an interface as edge (access ports)
docs.text:
[edit protocols rstp]
  interface ge-0/0/1 {
    edge;
    no-root-port;
  }

docs.title: Set interface as point-to-point (trunk ports)
docs.text:
[edit protocols rstp]
  interface ge-0/0/0 {
    mode point-to-point;
  }

docs.title: Bridge Priority Values
docs.text:
STP bridge priority must be a multiple of 4096:

Priority Value Hex Notes 4096 0x1000 Lowest common value — use for root bridge 8192 0x2000 Secondary root / backup root 32768 0x8000 Default — non-root bridges 61440 0xF000 Highest — never becomes root

docs.title: RSTP Port Roles and States
docs.text: Role State Meaning Root Forwarding Best path to root, active Designated Forwarding Best port on this segment toward root Alternate Discarding Redundant path, blocked Edge Forwarding End-host port, bypasses STP states

docs.title: Wrap-Up
docs.text:
After this session you have seen Layer 2 loop prevention in action. RSTP's proposal/agreement mechanism ensures fast sub-second failover — a critical property for production switching environments.

Session 4 returns to Layer 3 with OSPF on the SP core topology.

docs.title: Ping Still Fails After Enabling RSTP
docs.text:
Symptom: Pings between PCs fail even after commit on both switches.

Cause / Fix:

  1. RSTP needs a few seconds to converge on first enable — wait 5–10 seconds and retry
  2. Confirm RSTP is active: show spanning-tree bridge — if this returns empty, RSTP did not enable
  3. Confirm the bridge domain has RSTP enabled: show configuration bridge-domains VLAN10
  4. If the command syntax caused a commit error, refer to the guide corrections and re-apply

docs.title: show spanning-tree bridge Returns Nothing
docs.text:
Symptom: The command returns empty output or no spanning tree instances found.

Cause: RSTP was not successfully committed, or set protocols rstp was not applied.

Fix:

configure
set protocols rstp
commit
show spanning-tree bridge

Confirm the [edit protocols rstp] stanza exists in the configuration:

show configuration protocols rstp

If the stanza is missing, re-apply and commit.

docs.title: Broadcast Storm After Adding Second Trunk
docs.text:
Symptom: After adding the second trunk (Part 0), pings hang and the vMX becomes sluggish. show system processes shows high CPU.

Cause: Expected — this is what a broadcast storm looks like without STP.

Fix:

  1. From GNS3, right-click the second trunk link and delete or suspend it immediately
  2. Enable RSTP on both switches (Part 1) before reconnecting
  3. Reconnect the second trunk after RSTP is confirmed active

docs.title: One Trunk Port Stuck in Discarding After Failover
docs.text:
Symptom: After restoring the cut trunk link, the port remains in Discarding and does not return to Forwarding.

Cause / Fix:

RSTP should reconverge automatically. If it does not:

  1. Check the port is not administratively disabled: show interfaces ge-0/0/3 terse
  2. Force STP to recompute: disconnect and reconnect the link in GNS3
  3. On both switches, confirm RSTP is enabled and the bridge priority is correct: show spanning-tree bridge

docs.title: Access Ports Not Showing as Edge
docs.text:
Symptom: show spanning-tree interface shows access ports going through Discarding → Learning → Forwarding states on each link flap.

Cause: Edge port configuration was not applied.

Fix:

configure
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/1 edge
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/2 edge
commit

Apply on both switches. Edge ports skip the STP state machine entirely and go directly to Forwarding.

docs.title: Part 0 — Enable RSTP
docs.text: RSTP is enabled before adding the second trunk so the loop is never active without protection. This is the correct production approach — always enable spanning tree before introducing a redundant Layer 2 path.

docs.title: CLI Setup
docs.text:
Before pasting any configuration, run these two commands at the operational prompt (>) on each switch:

set cli screen-length 0
set cli complete-on-space off

Re-run these at the start of every console session — they do not persist across reconnects.

Pasting large config blocks

Use load set terminal in config mode instead of pasting directly at the # prompt. Junos buffers all input until you press Ctrl+D, then processes every set command at once — eliminating paste-rate issues with console terminals.

configure
load set terminal
set protocols rstp ...
^D
commit

docs.title: Step 1: Enable RSTP on SW1 (root bridge)
docs.text:
configure

set protocols rstp bridge-priority 4096
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/0
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/1 edge
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/2 edge

commit

docs.title: Step 2: Enable RSTP on SW2
docs.text:
configure

set protocols rstp bridge-priority 8k
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/0
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/1 edge
set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/2 edge

commit

8k is Junos shorthand for 8192. SW2's priority of 8192 is higher than SW1's 4096, so SW1 will win the root election once both trunks are active.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify RSTP is active
docs.text:
On both switches:

show spanning-tree bridge

Expected on SW1:

STP bridge parameters:
  Context: default-switch
  Enabled protocol: RSTP
  This bridge is the root

Expected on SW2:

  Enabled protocol: RSTP
  This bridge is not the root

Confirm RSTP is running on both switches before proceeding to Part 1.

docs.title: Part 1 — Add Second Trunk
docs.text: With RSTP already active, adding the second trunk creates a physical loop that RSTP will immediately detect and resolve by blocking one of the redundant paths. No broadcast storm will occur.

docs.title: Step 1: Draw the second trunk in GNS3
docs.text:
1. Click Add a link
  2. Click SW1 → select Adapter 5 (ge-0/0/3)
  3. Click SW2 → select Adapter 5 (ge-0/0/3)

The link appears in GNS3 but carries no traffic until configured on both switches.

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Trunk 2 on SW1
docs.text:
configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 flexible-vlan-tagging
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 10 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 10 vlan-id 10

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 11 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 11 vlan-id 11

set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/3.10
set bridge-domains VLAN11 interface ge-0/0/3.11

set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/3

commit

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Trunk 2 on SW2
docs.text:
configure

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 flexible-vlan-tagging
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 encapsulation flexible-ethernet-services

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 10 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 10 vlan-id 10

set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 11 encapsulation vlan-bridge
set interfaces ge-0/0/3 unit 11 vlan-id 11

set bridge-domains VLAN10 interface ge-0/0/3.10
set bridge-domains VLAN11 interface ge-0/0/3.11

set protocols rstp interface ge-0/0/3

commit

docs.title: Step 4: Verify RSTP blocked the redundant path
docs.text:
show spanning-tree interface

Expected on SW2 — one trunk port is Alternate/Discarding:

Interface      Port ID    Designated    Port    State       Role
                          bridge ID     Cost
ge-0/0/0.10    128:1      1000.SW1mac   2000  Forwarding  Root
ge-0/0/3.10    128:4      8000.SW2mac   2000  Discarding  Alternate

show bridge domain

Expected: both bridge domains now list three interfaces (two trunk subunits + one access port):

Routing instance        Bridge domain            VLAN ID     Interfaces
default-switch          VLAN10                   10
                                                     ge-0/0/0.10
                                                     ge-0/0/1.0
                                                     ge-0/0/3.10
default-switch          VLAN11                   11
                                                     ge-0/0/0.11
                                                     ge-0/0/2.0
                                                     ge-0/0/3.11

docs.title: Step 5: Confirm traffic is unaffected
docs.text:
From PC1, ping PC3:

ping 192.168.10.2

Pings should succeed without interruption — RSTP converged before the loop was ever active.

docs.title: Part 2 — Port Roles and States
docs.text: With RSTP converged, each port is assigned a role and a state. Understanding these is essential for troubleshooting STP-related connectivity issues.

docs.title: RSTP Port Roles
docs.text: Role (CLI) Description DESG — Designated The port on a segment providing the best path toward the root. All ports on the root bridge are Designated. ROOT — Root The port on a non-root bridge with the best path cost to the root bridge. One per switch. ALT — Alternate A blocked port providing a redundant path to the root. Transitions to Root if the active Root port fails.

docs.title: RSTP Port States
docs.text: State (CLI) Traffic forwarded? Description FWD — Forwarding Yes Normal operation BLK — Blocking No Redundant path suppressed by STP LRN — Learning No (data), Yes (MAC) Transitional — building MAC table

docs.title: Step 1: View port roles on SW1
docs.text:
show spanning-tree interface

Expected on SW1 (root bridge — all ports Designated/Forwarding):

Spanning tree interface parameters for instance 0

Interface    Port ID    Designated      Designated         Port    State  Role
                         port ID        bridge ID          Cost
ge-0/0/0         128:1        128:1   4096.0005867107d0     20000  FWD    DESG
ge-0/0/1         128:2        128:2   4096.0005867107d0     20000  FWD    DESG
ge-0/0/2         128:3        128:3   4096.0005867107d0     20000  FWD    DESG
ge-0/0/3         128:4        128:4   4096.0005867107d0     20000  FWD    DESG

The Designated bridge ID (4096.MAC) confirms SW1 is the root — the priority prefix 4096 matches the configured bridge-priority 4096. All of SW1's ports are DESG because the root bridge always wins on every segment.
